name: White Turf St. Moritz
tagline: Thoroughbreds thunder across a frozen alpine lake at 1,800 metres, champagne in hand and the Engadin peaks blazing white behind them.
dates: Sundays 7, 14 & 21 February 2027 (expected — follows the fixed three-Sundays-of-February pattern; official site lists the 2027 edition for these weekends)
history: White Turf was born in 1907, when St. Moritz's early winter-tourism pioneers staged the first official horse races on the frozen surface of Lake St. Moritz. The original impetus was skikjöring — a daring sport invented on this very lake in which a skier is towed behind a riderless thoroughbred at up to 50 km/h. The novelty of racing on ice in the middle of the Alps caught the world's imagination almost immediately: trotting races joined the programme in the earliest editions, and flat racing was added in 1911, giving the meeting the mixed card of gallop, trotting and skikjöring it still runs today.

Over more than a century the event evolved from a sporting curiosity into the most important equestrian meeting and social highlight of the alpine winter season. Its centrepiece, the Grand Prix of St. Moritz (long sponsored by Longines), is run as a kind of European championship on snow and has carried a symbolic purse of CHF 111,111 — the richest race in Switzerland. Racing on a living, frozen lake keeps nature in charge, and the event has been humbled by it: most recently the third Sunday of the 2017 edition was cancelled after a crack opened in the ice near the inner rail, a sobering reminder that the track itself is metres of frozen water over an alpine lake.

Today White Turf draws more than 30,000 spectators across its three February Sundays. It is as much a spectacle of the Belle Époque resort culture that created it as a race meeting: fur coats and champagne bars beside the rails, gourmet tents, art exhibitions and live music on the ice, with the village of St. Moritz — birthplace of alpine winter tourism and twice an Olympic host — rising on the hillside above. The skikjöring drivers, who compete across all three Sundays for the title 'King of the Engadin', remain the event's beating heart: it is the only place in the world where this form of the sport is raced.
whyGo: There is no other race meeting on earth like it: thoroughbreds and skikjöring daredevils at full gallop across a frozen lake, snow spraying against a backdrop of 3,000-metre peaks. It is a genuine one-off sporting spectacle wrapped in St. Moritz's glamour — and from Zürich it is an easy weekend, reachable via one of the world's most beautiful railway lines.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Chur (IC 3, ~1h15) → St. Moritz via the Rhaetian Railway Albula line (UNESCO World Heritage route, ~2h)
gettingThere.cost: CHF 60–130 return (from ~CHF 30 each way with Supersaver fares; half price with a Half-Fare Card)
gettingThere.notes: Book SBB Supersaver tickets up to 60 days out and sit on the right side Chur→St. Moritz for the Landwasser Viaduct. The journey through the Albula spirals is an attraction in itself.

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Chur (A3/A13) → Julier Pass → St. Moritz, ~200 km
gettingThere.cost: ~CHF 60–90 in fuel return, plus parking (Serletta car park near the lake fills early on race Sundays)
gettingThere.notes: The Julier Pass is kept open all winter but carry chains and check conditions; on race Sundays the train is honestly less stressful.

gettingThere.route: Zürich → Samedan/Engadin Airport (private charter or air taxi), 10 min transfer to St. Moritz
gettingThere.notes: Europe's highest airport (1,707 m) exists mainly for exactly this crowd. Purely a splurge option — there are no scheduled flights.

costs.notes: Estimates for rail from Zürich + 2 nights + one race Sunday. St. Moritz hotel rates in peak February are the main driver — save hugely by staying in Celerina, Samedan or Pontresina (10–15 min by train, included in some guest cards) or by day-tripping from Chur. Standing room on the ice is only CHF 25 and children under 16 are free; grandstand seats are CHF 45–85; VIP lounges and hotel packages push into four figures.

booking.label: Official site — White Turf St. Moritz

weather: High-alpine February: typically -10 to 0°C, often brilliantly sunny (the Engadin claims ~300 sun days a year) but you are standing on a frozen lake for hours — wind off the ice bites. Pack serious insulated boots, layered wool, sunglasses and high-SPF sunscreen; the glare off snow at 1,800 m is fierce.
tips: The three Sundays differ in character: the Grand Prix of St. Moritz (the richest race) runs on the final Sunday, which is also the busiest — the first Sunday is the most relaxed for a first visit.
Standing access to the ice is only CHF 25 (children free) and gets you remarkably close to the rail; you don't need a grandstand seat to have a spectacular day.
Races run roughly midday to mid-afternoon; arrive by 10:30 to wander the tents, watch the parade ring and place a small tote bet before the crowds thicken.
Book accommodation 3–6 months out — February is absolute peak season in St. Moritz; staying in Celerina or Pontresina cuts hotel costs by half for a 10-minute train ride.
Buy SBB Supersaver fares when they open (60 days ahead) and consider a Half-Fare Card if you'll make multiple Swiss trips during your year in Zürich — it pays for itself in 2–3 journeys.
Don't miss the skikjöring heats — it exists nowhere else in the world — and dress far warmer than you think you need; you're on lake ice, not a city street.

localRecs.name: Hanselmann
localRecs.note: St. Moritz's grand 19th-century konditorei opposite the lake — warm up with hot chocolate and Engadiner Nusstorte, the local walnut caramel tart.

localRecs.name: Kulm Hotel Sunny Bar
localRecs.note: One of the Alps' oldest sports bars, plastered with Cresta Run memorabilia; the Kulm's founder Johannes Badrutt essentially invented winter tourism here in 1864.

localRecs.name: Olympia Bob Run & Cresta Run
localRecs.note: The world's last natural-ice bobsleigh run, St. Moritz to Celerina — watch runs (or book a guest taxi ride) on a non-race morning.

localRecs.name: Muottas Muragl
localRecs.note: Funicular up to 2,456 m between Samedan and Pontresina for the definitive sunset panorama over the frozen Engadin lakes.

localRecs.name: Pontresina
localRecs.note: Quieter, prettier and cheaper valley village 15 minutes away — good base with serious mountain restaurants and the Morteratsch glacier walk nearby.
